A Proof of ResultsA Proof of Results1.1 Proof of Proposition 3.1: Polarized Coherent Equilibria (\(m > \epsilon \))For \(m>\epsilon \), (13) can be rewritten as$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0):=1-m - \frac{1-m_0}{\epsilon ^2 T^2} \varphi \left( \frac{1+mT}{\epsilon \, T} \right) = 0, \end{aligned}$$
                    (19)
                where$$\begin{aligned} \varphi (y) = \frac{y^2 + 1}{(y^2-1)^2}. \end{aligned}$$Before describing the solutions of the equation \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) \) in \((\epsilon ,1]\), we give some simple facts that will be useful in the proof.Fact 1. The map \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly concave for \(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\), for each \(\epsilon ,T,m_0\).
Proof
This comes immediately from the fact that \(\varphi \) is strictly convex in \((1,+\infty )\). Indeed,$$\begin{aligned} \varphi '(y) = - \frac{2y(3+y^2)}{(y^2-1)^3}, \ \ \ \varphi ''(y) = \frac{6(y^4 + 6 y^2 + 1)}{(y^2-1)^4}. \end{aligned}$$
                    (20)
                Note also that \(\varphi \) is strictly decreasing. \(\square \)
As a consequence of Fact 1, (19) can have at most two solutions in \((\epsilon ,1]\).Fact 2. The map \(T \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly increasing for \(T \in [0,+\infty )\), for each \(m,\epsilon ,m_0\), with \(m > \epsilon \).
Proof
To see this, note that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial F}{\partial T} (m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{2(1-m_0)\left[ m(1+Tm)^3 - 3 \epsilon ^2 T(1+Tm) - \epsilon ^4 T^3\right] }{\left( (1+Tm)^3 - \epsilon ^2 T^2\right) ^3}, \end{aligned}$$which has the same sign as \( h(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0):= m(1+Tm)^3 - 3 \epsilon ^2 T(1+Tm) - \epsilon ^4 T^3. \) Observing that \(h(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \epsilon \), that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial h}{\partial m} (m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = (1+Tm)^3 + 3 Tm(1+Tm)^2 - 3 \epsilon ^2 T^2 \end{aligned}$$is increasing in m and \( \frac{\partial h}{\partial m} (\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = 4 \epsilon ^3 T^3 + 6 \epsilon ^2 T^2 + 6 \epsilon \, T + 1 >0, \) we conclude that \(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T} (m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)>0\) for \(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\). This proves Fact2. \(\square \)
Fact 3. \(\forall m,\epsilon ,m_0\), with \(m > \epsilon ,\)$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,0^+,m_0):=\lim _{T \downarrow 0} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = m_0 - m \end{aligned}$$and$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{T \uparrow +\infty } F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{1+m_0-2\epsilon }{2}, \end{aligned}$$whereas for \(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\),$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{T \uparrow +\infty } F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = 1-m. \end{aligned}$$These are simple asymptotics, and the proof is omitted.Now, we can prove Proposition 3.1. We begin with the case \(\epsilon \le m_0\). By Fact 3, \( F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,0^+,m_0) = m_0 - \epsilon \ge 0; \) thus, by Fact 2, \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)>0\), \(\forall T>0\). Since, clearly, \(F(1,\epsilon ,T,m_0)<0\) and \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly concave by Fact 1, the uniqueness of the solution to (19) follows readily and case (i) is proved.Now, consider the case \(\epsilon \ge \frac{1+m_0}{2}\) (which implies \(\epsilon >m_0\)). By Facts 2 and 3, \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)< 0\), \(\forall T>0\). Since \(F(m,\epsilon ,0^+,m_0) = m_0 - m\), by continuity (19) has no solution in \((\epsilon ,1]\) for T small enough. Now, we claim that there exists a unique \(T_c^{(1)} = T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)>0\) such that the graph of \(z= F(m,\epsilon ,T_c^{(1)},m_0)\) is tangent to the line \(z=0\). Since \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly increasing in T (Fact 2), such \(T_c^{(1)}\), if any, is unique. To prove the existence of \(T_c^{(1)}\) by continuity, it is enough to show that there are values of \(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\) and \(T>0\) such that \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)>0\): this is true \(\forall m \in (\epsilon ,1)\) as \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) \rightarrow 1-m\) for \(T \uparrow +\infty \) (Fact 3). The conclusions in case (ii) are now obvious consequences of concavity and T-monotonicity of F.Consider, finally, the case \(m_0< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\). We first investigate the following equation in the unknown \(T>0\):$$\begin{aligned} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)=0, \end{aligned}$$
                    (21)
                that can be rewritten as$$\begin{aligned} 1-\epsilon - \frac{2\epsilon ^2 T^2 + 2 \epsilon \,T + 1}{4 \epsilon ^2 T^2 + 4 \epsilon \,T + 1} (1-m_0) = 0. \end{aligned}$$This can be cast as a quadratic equation in T: it has real solutions if and only if \(\epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), and in this case the only positive solution is \(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\) given in (15). Note that \(\epsilon > m_0\) implies that \(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)>0\). Now, T-monotonicity and m-concavity of \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) and the fact that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = -1+2(1-m_0) \frac{T(\epsilon \,T+1)(4 \epsilon ^2 T^2 + 2 \epsilon \, T + 1)}{2\epsilon \, T + 1} \end{aligned}$$
                    (22)
                is strictly increasing in T, show that there are two alternatives: 

(a)
if \(\frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) \le 0\), then,

for \(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\), the map \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is less than or equal to 0 at \(m = \epsilon \) and it is decreasing: there are no solutions to (19) in \((\epsilon ,1]\);


for \(T > T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\), the map \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly positive at \(m = \epsilon \), concave and negative at \(m=1\), so (19) has a unique solution in \((\epsilon ,1]\);




(b)
if \(\frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) > 0\), then by continuity there exists \(T_c^{(1)} = T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0) \in (0,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0))\) such that the graph of \(z= F(m,\epsilon ,T_c,m_0)\) is tangent to the line \(z=0\); as above, this \(T_c^{(1)}\) is unique. Thus,

for \(T<T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\), (19) has no solutions in \((\epsilon ,1]\);


for \(T=T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\), (19) has a unique solution in \((\epsilon ,1]\);


for \(T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)<T<T^*(\epsilon , m_0)\), (19) has two solutions in \((\epsilon ,1]\);


for \(T \ge T^*(\epsilon , m_0)\), (19) has a unique solution in \((\epsilon ,1]\).



Note that \(T_c^{(1)}\) is defined as in case (ii), and by the Implicit Function Theorem it is continuous at \(\epsilon \), \(\forall \epsilon \) in its domain. To complete the proof of case (iii), we are left to show that there exists \(\epsilon _*^{(1)} \in \left( m_0,\frac{1+m_0}{2}\right) \) such that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) > 0 \ \ \hbox { if and only if } \ \ \epsilon \in \left( \epsilon _*^{(1)},\frac{1+m_0}{2}\right) , \end{aligned}$$so that \(T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\) is defined for \(\epsilon \in (\epsilon _*^{(1)},1]\). Moreover, if this is the case, continuity implies that$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{\epsilon \downarrow \epsilon _*^{(1)}} T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)= T^*(\epsilon ^*,m_0). \end{aligned}$$To complete the proof we are left to show the existence of such \(\epsilon _*^{(1)}\). This is established by proving that the map \( \epsilon \mapsto \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) \) is strictly increasing, negative at \(\epsilon = m_0\) and diverging to \(+\infty \) at \(\epsilon = \frac{1+m_0}{2}\). We use the expressions (22) and (15), and the change of variable \( y:= 1 + 2 \epsilon \, T^*(\epsilon ). \) Note that, by (15),$$\begin{aligned} \epsilon \,T^*(\epsilon ) = - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{1-m_0}{1+m_0-2\epsilon }}, \end{aligned}$$so \(y = \sqrt{\frac{1-m_0}{1+m_0-2\epsilon }}\). It is easily seen that \(\frac{dy}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } > 0\), \(y(m_0) = 1\) and \( \lim _{\epsilon \uparrow \frac{1+m_0}{2}} y(\epsilon ) = +\infty . \)We can write, using (22),$$\begin{aligned} G(\epsilon ,m_0):= \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) = -1 + (1-m_0) \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{2 \epsilon y}. \end{aligned}$$This shows that \(G(m_0,m_0) = -1\) and that \(G(\epsilon ,m_0)\) diverges to \(+\infty \) as \(\epsilon \uparrow \frac{1+m_0}{2}\). So the last step is to prove that G is strictly increasing in \(\epsilon \). We have$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial G}{\partial \epsilon }(\epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{1-m_0}{2\epsilon } \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}y} \left( \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \right) \frac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } - \frac{1-m_0}{2 \epsilon ^2} \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y}. \end{aligned}$$Using the facts that \( \frac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } = \frac{y^3}{1-m_0} \hbox { and } \epsilon = \frac{(1+m_0)y^2 - (1-m_0)}{y^2}, \) it follows that \(\frac{\partial G}{\partial \epsilon }(\epsilon ,m_0) \) has the same sign as$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} \frac{\epsilon y^3}{1-m_0} \frac{3 y^4 - 2 y^3 - y^2 + 1}{y^2} - \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \nonumber \\{} & {} \quad = \frac{\frac{1+m_0}{1-m_0} y^2 - 1}{y}(y^2(y-1)(3y+1) + 1) - \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \nonumber \\{} & {} \quad \ge \frac{ y^2 - 1}{y}(y^2(y-1)(3y+1) + 1) - \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \nonumber \\{} & {} \quad = (y^2 - 1)(y-1)(3y^2 +y - 1)> 0, \ \ \forall \, y>1, \end{aligned}$$
                    (23)
                where we have used the fact that \(m_0 \ge 0\) and the expression in the second line of (23) is increasing in \(m_0\). This completes the proof. \(\square \)1.2 Proof of Proposition 3.2: Polarized Incoherent Equilibria (\(m < -\epsilon \))The proof repeats some of the arguments seen in the proof of Proposition 3.1. It is convenient to take advantage of the symmetry relation (14). This implies that we can equivalently find the equilibria in \((\epsilon ,1]\) after replacing \(m_0\) by \(-m_0\). For the regime \(\epsilon \ge \frac{1-m_0}{2}\), the proof of part (ii) of Proposition 3.1 applies with no changes, as the assumption \(m_0 \ge 0\) was not used. For the case \(0< \epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}\), we can adapt the proof of part (iii) of Proposition 3.1, where the assumption \(m_0 \ge 0\) was only used to prove the existence of \(\epsilon _*^{(1)}\). Here, we obtain the same behavior seen for \(\epsilon _*^{(1)}< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\) in Proposition 3.1: to repeat the same argument we need to show that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0),-m_0) > 0, \ \ \ \forall \epsilon : \ \ 0< \epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}. \end{aligned}$$Indeed, as seen in the proof of Proposition 3.1,$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0),-m_0) = -1 + (1+m_0) \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{2 \epsilon y}, \end{aligned}$$with \(y = \sqrt{\frac{1+m_0}{1-m_0-2\epsilon }}\). Note that, as \(m_0 \ge 0\), \(y >1\) for all \( 0< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\). In particular, \(y^2-y+1>1\) so that, with a further simple computation we get$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0),-m_0)> -1+ (1+m_0) \frac{y^2-1}{2 \epsilon y} = -1 + \frac{\epsilon + m_0}{\epsilon }y >0. \end{aligned}$$Thus, the proof for the case \(0< \epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}\) can be carried out in the same way as the proof of part (iii) of Proposition 3.1 (case \(\epsilon _*^{(1)}< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)). \(\square \)1.3 Proof of Proposition 3.3: Unpolarized Coherent Equilibria (\(0\le m\le \epsilon \))For \(m\in [0,\epsilon ]\), equation (13) becomes:$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0):= \frac{2(\epsilon \,T+1)Tm + m_0 \left[ (\epsilon \, T+1)^2 + m^2 T^2 \right] }{\left( (\epsilon \, T+1)^2 - m^2 T^2\right) ^2} - m = 0. \end{aligned}$$
                    (24)
                We begin by observing that$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{1}{(\epsilon \, T+1)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{T}{1+\epsilon \, T} m, m_0\right) - m, \end{aligned}$$
                    (25)
                where$$\begin{aligned} \varphi (z,m_0):= \frac{2z+m_0 (1+z^2)}{(1-z^2)^2}. \end{aligned}$$
                    (26)
                In particular, this allows to prove easily that, if \(m_0 \ge 0\), then \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is convex. 

(i)
Note first that \( F(0,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{m_0}{(\epsilon \,T +1)^2} > 0 \)

(recall that here \(m_0 \ge \epsilon >0\)). Moreover,$$\begin{aligned} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{2(\epsilon \,T + 1) \epsilon \,T + m_0 \left[ (\epsilon \,T + 1) + \epsilon ^2 T^2\right] }{\left( (\epsilon \, T + 1)^2 - \epsilon ^2 T^2\right) ^2} - \epsilon = \psi (\epsilon \,T,m_0) - \epsilon , \end{aligned}$$with$$\begin{aligned} \psi (y):= \frac{2(y+1)y + m_0 \left[ (y+1)^2 + y^2 \right] }{(2y+1)^2}. \end{aligned}$$As \( \psi '(y) = \frac{2(1-m_0)}{(2y+1)^3}, \) we deduce that \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly increasing in T, except for \(m_0=1\) (in this case it is constant). In all cases, we have that, since \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,0,m_0) = m_0-\epsilon \ge 0\), \( F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) >0, \) \(\forall T>0\). Thus, the map \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly positive at the endpoints of the interval \((0,\epsilon )\). We also have \( F(m,\epsilon ,0,m_0) = m_0 - m >0 \) \(\forall m \in (0,\epsilon )\). Moreover, it is easily seen that for each \(m \in (0,\epsilon )\),$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = -m <0. \end{aligned}$$
                    (27)
                Then, there must be a time \(T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\) such that for \(T < T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\), \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) > 0\), \(\forall m \in (0,\epsilon )\), and the graph of the convex function \(y = F(m,\epsilon ,T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0)\) is tangent to the line \(y=0\). Set$$\begin{aligned} {\mathcal {T}}:= \left\{ T > 0: \min _{m \in (0,\epsilon )}F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) \le 0 \right\} . \end{aligned}$$The proof of this point (i) is completed as we show that \({\mathcal {T}} = [T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0), +\infty )\). If this is not the case, by continuity, there must be a time \({\hat{T}} \ge T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\), \({\hat{m}} \in (0,\epsilon )\) and \(\delta >0\) such that$$\begin{aligned} F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,{\hat{T}},m_0) = 0, \ \ \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}m}F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,{\hat{T}},m_0) = 0, \end{aligned}$$
                    (28)
                but \( F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,t,m_0)>0, \) \(\forall t \in ({\hat{T}}, {\hat{T}}+\delta )\). To show that this is impossible, it is enough to prove that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}T}F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,{\hat{T}},m_0) <0. \end{aligned}$$
                    (29)
                To see this, it is convenient to perform the following change of variables: \(u:= \frac{m}{\epsilon } \in (0,1)\) and \(r:= \epsilon \, T\), so that$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) - \epsilon u, \end{aligned}$$
                    (30)
                where \(\varphi \) is given in (26). Note that (29) is equivalent to$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}r} G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) <0, \end{aligned}$$
                    (31)
                where \({\hat{u}}:= \frac{{\hat{m}}}{\epsilon }\) and \({\hat{r}}:= \epsilon {\hat{T}}\).
Claim
\({\hat{r}} > \frac{1}{2}\).
Proof
To see this, note that, being \(\epsilon \le m_0\),$$\begin{aligned} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) \ge H(u,r,m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) - m_0 u. \end{aligned}$$The claim follows if we show that, \(\forall r \le \frac{1}{2}\),$$\begin{aligned} H(u,r,m_0) > 0, \ \ \forall u \in (0,1). \end{aligned}$$
                    (32)
                Since \(H(u,r,m_0)\) is linear in \(m_0\), it is enough to prove (32) for \(m_0\in \{0,1\}\). For \(m_0 = 0\) this is obvious, so we show it for \(m_0 = 1\):$$\begin{aligned} H(u,r,1) = \frac{\left( 1+ \frac{r}{1+r} u \right) ^2}{(1+r)^2 \left( 1- \frac{r^2 u^2}{(1+r)^2} \right) ^2} - u = \frac{(1-u)(r^2 u^2 - (r^2 +2r)u+1)}{(1+r(1-u))^2}, \end{aligned}$$which has the same sign as \( p(u):= r^2 u^2 - (r^2 +2r)u+1). \) If \(r \le \frac{1}{2}\) (indeed here \(r<2\) would suffice), \(p'(u) = 2 r^2 u - r^2 - 2r < 0\), \(\forall u \in (0,1)\), so, \( p(u) \ge p(1) = 1-2r \ge 0, \) \(\forall u \in (0,1)\) and for \(r \le \frac{1}{2}\). This completes the proof of the Claim. \(\square \)
We are now left with the proof of (31). Note that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}r}G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = - \frac{2}{(1+r)^3} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) + \frac{u}{(1+r)^4} \varphi '\left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) , \end{aligned}$$
                    (33)
                where \( \varphi '(z,m_0) = \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}z} \varphi (z,m_0). \) By (28),$$\begin{aligned} G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) = 0 \ \Rightarrow \ \varphi \left( \frac{{\hat{r}}}{1+{\hat{r}}} {\hat{u}}, m_0\right) = \epsilon {\hat{u}}, \end{aligned}$$and$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u}G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) = 0 \ \Rightarrow \ \frac{r}{(1+r)^3} \varphi '\left( \frac{{\hat{r}}}{1+{\hat{r}}} {\hat{u}}, m_0\right) = \epsilon . \end{aligned}$$Inserting these identities in (33), we obtain$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}r}G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) = \frac{\epsilon {\hat{u}}}{1+{\hat{r}}}\left( -2+\frac{1}{{\hat{r}}} \right) <0, \quad \text { as } {\hat{r}} > \frac{1}{2}. \end{aligned}$$

(ii)
Note that \(F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{m_0}{(\epsilon \, T+1)^2} >0\). Moreover, \( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{1+m_0}{2} - \epsilon \le 0. \) Since, as shown in point (i), the map \(T \mapsto F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) \) is strictly increasing, then \( F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) <0, \) \(\forall T >0\). Thus, \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\) has opposite sign at the endpoints of \((0,\epsilon )\): by convexity there is a unique solution \(m = M(T, \epsilon ,m_0)\) to \(F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0\). The fact that \( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } M(T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \) follows from the fact that \( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \) and that \( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}m} F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = -1. \)


(iii)
In this case, \(F(0,T, \epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{m_0}{(\epsilon \, T +1)^2} \ge 0\). Moreover, \(F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0)< 0 \ \iff \ T < T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),\) as already seen in the proof of Proposition 3.1, point (iii). By convexity of \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\), for \(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\) Eq. (24) has a unique solution in \([0,\epsilon )\). The fact that \(m=0\) is a solution if and only if \(m_0=0\) is easily verified. Moreover, by (27), for T sufficiently large \(F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\) attains negative values, it is positive at \(m=0\) and \(m=\epsilon \), so (24) has two solutions in \([0,\epsilon )\). We need, however, a sharper analysis for \(T> T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\). Note that, in this case \(F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) > 0\) and \(F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) >0\). Thus, by convexity of \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\), (24) has zero or two solutions, except for the “special times” T for which the graph of the map \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\) is tangent to the horizontal axis. Note that these special times are identified as (T-component of the) solutions in \((0,\epsilon ) \times (T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),+\infty )\) of the system $$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \\ \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}m}F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$
                    (34)
                 Note that (34) has no solutions with \(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\), so we may look for solutions of (34) in \((0,\epsilon ) \times (0,+\infty )\). The remaining part of the proof is based on the following Lemma.


Lemma A.1
Let \(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\) and \(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\) be as in the statement of Proposition 3.3. Then,$$\begin{aligned} m_0< \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)< \epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0) < \frac{1+m_0}{2}, \end{aligned}$$(unless for \(m_0=0\), where \(0 = \epsilon _*^{(2)}(0)< \epsilon _*^{(3)}(0) < \frac{1}{2}\)) such that 

(a)
for \(m_0 < \epsilon \le \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\), (34) has a unique solution \(({\tilde{m}}(\epsilon ,m _0), {\tilde{T}}(\epsilon ,m_0))\);


(b)
for \(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)< \epsilon <\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\), (34) has two solutions \(({\hat{m}}(\epsilon ,m _0), T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0))\) and \(({\tilde{m}}(\epsilon ,m _0), T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0))\) with \( T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)< T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\);


(c)
for \( \epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0) \le \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), (34) has no solutions.


The proof of this lemma is postponed after the end of this section. The desired result of the solutions of (24) readily follows from this Lemma. Indeed, in case (a), there is a unique special time \(T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\). Since, for large T, (24) has two solutions, necessarily for \(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0) < T \le T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\) we must have \(F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)>0\), \(\forall m \in (0,\epsilon )\), so (24) has no solution.In case (b), there are two special times \( T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)< T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\): the only possibility is that, for \(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)< T < T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\), the graph of \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\) crosses twice the horizontal axis (two solutions for (24)), for \(T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)< T<T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\) it stays above the horizontal axis (no solutions for (24)) and it crosses again twice the horizontal axis for \(T > T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\). The proof is therefore completed. \(\square \)1.4 Proof of Lemma A.1
By the change of variables \(u:= \frac{m}{\epsilon } \in (0,1)\) and \(r:= \epsilon \, T\) as in (30), we may replace \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) by$$\begin{aligned} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r+ru)^2} \left[ m_0 + \frac{2(1+m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \right] - \epsilon u =0, \end{aligned}$$\(u \in (0,1)\), \(r >0\), and (34) is equivalent to$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0 \\ \frac{\text {d}}{\text {d}u} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$
                    (35)
                Letting, as above, \( U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= m_0(1+r-ru)^2 + 2(1+m_0)r(1+r)u - \epsilon u\left[ (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right] ^2 = 0, \) we have that \( G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = \frac{1}{\left[ (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right] ^2} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0). \) It is immediately seen that (35) is equivalent to$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0 \\ \frac{\text {d}}{\text {d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0, \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$
                    (36)
                We use again the identity \( U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) +\left( m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) \left( (1+r)^2 - u^2 r^2 \right) , \) which implies that (36) is equivalent to$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \left( m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) = 0 \\ \frac{\text {d}}{\text {d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$
                    (37)
                Summing up, the number of pairs \((m,T) \in (0,\epsilon ) \times (0,+\infty )\) solving (34) equals the number of solutions r to the equation$$\begin{aligned} W(r,\epsilon ,m_0):= \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U\left( \left( \frac{m_0}{4 \epsilon r^2} \right) ^{1/3},\epsilon ,r,m_0\right) = 0 \end{aligned}$$
                    (38)
                such that \(\frac{m_0}{4 \epsilon r^2} <1\), i.e., \(r> \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }}\). With the help of a symbolic calculator, we obtain$$\begin{aligned} 8W(r,\epsilon ,m_0)= & {} r \left\{ 16 + \left[ 16 + 3m_0 \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon r^2} \right) ^{1/3} \right] r \right\} \\{} & {} \quad + 4 \epsilon (1+r)^2 \left\{ -2-4r+ \left[ -2 + 3 \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon r^2} \right) ^{1/3}\right] r^2 \right\} . \end{aligned}$$Now, set \( V(s,\epsilon ,m_0):= W(\sqrt{s},\epsilon ,m_0). \) Thus, we are left with the problem of finding the solutions of$$\begin{aligned} V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \end{aligned}$$
                    (39)
                with \(s > \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }\). This last change of variable is a trick to get the following claim.
Claim
The map \(s \mapsto V(s,\epsilon ,m_0)\) is strictly concave in \(\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon },+\infty \right) \).
Proof
Using again a symbolic calculator we get, letting \(k:= \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{1/3}\),$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} -12 s^{3/2} \frac{\textrm{d}^2}{\textrm{d}s^2}V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) \\{} & {} \qquad = 6+m_0 k s^{1/6} + \epsilon \left[ - 12 + 4 k^2 s^{-1/6} + 36 s + 5 k^2 s^{1/3} + 24 s^{3/2} - 8 k^2 s^{5/6} \right] . \end{aligned}$$To prove the claim, we need to show that this last expression is positive. Note that \(s > \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon } = \left( \frac{k}{2}\right) ^3\). Moreover, as \(m_0< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), then \( \frac{4m_0}{1+m_0}< k^3 < 2. \) Again from \(\epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), the inequality \( \frac{\textrm{d}^2}{\textrm{d}s^2}V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) <0\) follows if we show that$$\begin{aligned} 6+m_0 k s^{1/6} + \frac{1+m_0}{2} \left[ - 12 + 4 k^2 s^{-1/6} + 36 s + 5 k^2 s^{1/3} + 24 s^{3/2} - 8 k^2 s^{5/6} \right] >0. \end{aligned}$$
                    (40)
                Using \(s > \left( \frac{k}{2}\right) ^3\) and \(k^3 > \frac{4m_0}{1+m_0}\), we have that \( \frac{1+m_0}{2}5 k^2\,s^{1/3}>\frac{1+m_0}{4}5k^3> 5m_0, \) so the l.h.s. of (40) is bounded from below by$$\begin{aligned} m_0 k s^{1/6} - m_0 + (1+m_0) \left[ 2k^2 s^{-1/6} + 18 s + 12 s^{3/2} - 4 k^2 s^{5/6} \right] . \end{aligned}$$
                    (41)
                Now, for \(s \le \frac{1}{4}\) the expression in (41) is bounded from below by$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} - m_0 + (1+m_0) \left[ 2k^2 s^{-1/6} - 4 k^2 s^{5/6} \right]&= -m_0 +\frac{1+m_0}{s^{1/6}} \left( 2k^2 - 4 k^2 s \right) \\ {}&\ge - m_0 +(1+m_0) k^2 s^{-1/6} \\ {}&\ge - m_0 +(1+m_0) \left( \frac{4 m_0}{1+m_0} \right) ^{2/3} \\ {}&\ge - m_0 + (1+m_0) 4^{2/3} \frac{m_0}{1+m_0} >0. \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$For \(s > \frac{1}{4}\), the expression in (41) is bounded from below by$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} - m_0 + 18 s + 12 s^{3/2} - 4 k^2 s^{5/6}&\ge -m_0 + s^{5/6} \left[ 18 \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{1/6} + 12 \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{2/3} - 4 k^2 \right] \\ {}&\ge -m_0 + s^{5/6} \left[ \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{1/6} + 12 \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{2/3} - 4 \, 2^{2/3} \right] \\ {}&\ge -m_0 + 8s^{5/6} \ge -m_0 + 2 > 0. \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$This proves (40) and therefore the claim. \(\square \)
Now, using concavity of \(V(s,\epsilon ,m_0)\) and the fact that \( \lim _{s \rightarrow +\infty } V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) = \lim _{r \rightarrow +\infty } W(r,\epsilon ,m_0) = -\infty , \) we have that (39) has a unique solution whenever \( V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) >0. \) We get$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) = \frac{m_0(1+m_0)}{2\epsilon } + (1+m_0) \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }} - \epsilon \left( 1+e \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }} \right) , \end{aligned}$$so$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) \Big |_{\epsilon = m_0} = \frac{3}{2}(1-m_0) >0, \end{aligned}$$$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) \Big |_{\epsilon = \frac{(1+m_0)}{2}} = -\frac{1}{2}(1-m_0) < 0 \end{aligned}$$
                    (42)
                and$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) = -1- \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }}-\frac{m_0(1+m_0)}{2\epsilon ^2} - \frac{ \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }}(1+m_0)}{2\epsilon } < 0. \end{aligned}$$Therefore, there is a unique \(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\), with \(m_0< \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0) < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), such that \(V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon _*^{(2)}}, \epsilon _*^{(2)}, m_0\right) = 0\). Moreover, for \(m_0< \epsilon < \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\), (39) has a unique solution and, since$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}s} V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) \Big |_{s = \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }} = \frac{2(1+m_0-2\epsilon ) \left( 1+\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\alpha }}\right) }{\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\alpha }}} >0, \end{aligned}$$it follows that (39) has two solutions as \(\epsilon \) crosses \(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\). This actually occurs until \(\epsilon \) reaches \(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\), where \(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\) is characterized by the fact that the graph of \(s \mapsto V(s,\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0),m_0)\) is tangent to the horizontal axis. This is a consequence of the following monotonicity property:$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) <0,\quad \forall s \ge \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }. \end{aligned}$$
                    (43)
                This suffices to characterize \(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\); to complete the proof, we need to show that \(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0) < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), which is equivalent to$$\begin{aligned} V\left( s,\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) <0,\quad \forall s \ge \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)}. \end{aligned}$$
                    (44)
                We are therefore left to prove (43) and (44). We begin with (43).$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) \\{} & {} \quad =-\frac{1}{8\epsilon } \left[ m_0 \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{1/3} s^{2/3} + 4 \epsilon (1+\sqrt{s})^2 \left( 2 + 4 \sqrt{s} + 4 s -\left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{2/3} s^{1/3}\right) \right] . \end{aligned}$$To show that this expression is negative, we just observe that, being \(\frac{2m_0}{\epsilon } \le 2\),$$\begin{aligned} 2 + 4 \sqrt{s} + 4 s -\left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{2/3} s^{1/3}> 2 + 4 \sqrt{s} + 4 s -2 s^{1/3}> \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} 2-2s^{1/3} \ge 0 &{} \hbox {for } s \le 1 \\ 4s - 2 s^{1/3}> 0 &{} \hbox {for } s>1. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$This establishes (43). Now, we show (44). We recall that \(s \mapsto V\left( s,\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) \) is strictly concave for \(s \ge \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)}\). Moreover, again with the help of a symbolic calculator$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}s} V\left( s,\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) \Big |_{s = \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)}} = 0. \end{aligned}$$So it is enough to show that$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)},\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) <0, \end{aligned}$$that has been seen already in (42). The proof is now complete. \(\square \)1.5 Proof of Proposition 3.4: Unpolarized Incoherent Equilibria (\(-\epsilon \le m < 0\))As done in Proposition 3.2, we use the symmetry (14). So we look for solutions \(m \in (0,\epsilon ]\) of the equation \( F(m,\epsilon ,T,-m_0) = 0; \) this allows to reuse some of the ideas in Proposition 3.3. 

(i)
We employ here the change of variables seen in (30), so $$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,-m_0)&= G(u,\epsilon ,r,-m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, -m_0\right) - \epsilon u \\ {}&= \frac{2(1+r)ru-m_0\left[ (1+r)^2+r^2 u^2\right] }{\left( (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right) ^2} - \epsilon u \\&= \frac{1}{(1+r+ru)^2} \left[ -m_0 + \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \right] - \epsilon u. \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$ We need to show that this last expression is strictly negative \(\forall u\in (0,1)\); it is enough to show this for \(\epsilon = \frac{1-m_0}{2}\). This amounts to prove that $$\begin{aligned} -m_0 + \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} < \frac{1-m_0}{2} u (1+r+ru)^2, \end{aligned}$$ which follows from $$\begin{aligned}{} & {} \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \\{} & {} \quad< \frac{1-m_0}{2} u (1+r+ru)^2 \\{} & {} \qquad \iff \ 4r(1+r) < \left[ (1+r)^2 - r^2 u^2 \right] ^2, \end{aligned}$$\(\forall u \in (0,1)\). This last inequality follows if we show it holds for \(u=1\), i.e., \( 4r(1+r) < (1+2r)^2, \) that is clearly true \(\forall r>0\).


(ii)
As seen in the proof of point (i) of Proposition 3.3, \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,-m_0)\) is strictly increasing in T. Moreover, the same computation done after (21) shows that \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,-m_0)>0\) if and only if \(T>T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\). By the same change of variables used in point (i), (24) is equivalent to the equation $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{(1+r+ru)^2} \left[ -m_0 + \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \right] - \epsilon u =0, \end{aligned}$$ with \(u \in (0,1)\), which is also equivalent to $$\begin{aligned}{} & {} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= -m_0(1+r-ru)^2 \\{} & {} \qquad + 2(1-m_0)r(1+r)u - \epsilon u\left[ (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right] ^2 = 0. \end{aligned}$$ Our proof is based on the following claim.


Claim
Let \(u^* \in (0,1)\) be such that \(U(u^*,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0\). Then, \( \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u^*,\epsilon ,r,m_0)>0. \)
Proof
We use the identity$$\begin{aligned} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0)= & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) +\left( -m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) \left( (1+r)^2 - u^2 r^2 \right) \\< & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) \end{aligned}$$which proves the claim. \(\square \)
This clearly implies that such \(u^*\) exists if and only if \(U(1,\epsilon ,r,m_0) >0\) (as \(U(0,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = -m_0(1+r)^2\le 0\)), i.e., if and only if \(r > \epsilon \, T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\) and in this case it is unique. This completes the proof. \(\square \)

text_blocks.text:
A Proof of Results1.1 Proof of Proposition 3.1: Polarized Coherent Equilibria (\(m > \epsilon \))For \(m>\epsilon \), (13) can be rewritten as$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0):=1-m - \frac{1-m_0}{\epsilon ^2 T^2} \varphi \left( \frac{1+mT}{\epsilon \, T} \right) = 0, \end{aligned}$$
                    (19)
                where$$\begin{aligned} \varphi (y) = \frac{y^2 + 1}{(y^2-1)^2}. \end{aligned}$$Before describing the solutions of the equation \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) \) in \((\epsilon ,1]\), we give some simple facts that will be useful in the proof.Fact 1. The map \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly concave for \(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\), for each \(\epsilon ,T,m_0\).
Proof
This comes immediately from the fact that \(\varphi \) is strictly convex in \((1,+\infty )\). Indeed,$$\begin{aligned} \varphi '(y) = - \frac{2y(3+y^2)}{(y^2-1)^3}, \ \ \ \varphi ''(y) = \frac{6(y^4 + 6 y^2 + 1)}{(y^2-1)^4}. \end{aligned}$$
                    (20)
                Note also that \(\varphi \) is strictly decreasing. \(\square \)
As a consequence of Fact 1, (19) can have at most two solutions in \((\epsilon ,1]\).Fact 2. The map \(T \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly increasing for \(T \in [0,+\infty )\), for each \(m,\epsilon ,m_0\), with \(m > \epsilon \).
Proof
To see this, note that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial F}{\partial T} (m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{2(1-m_0)\left[ m(1+Tm)^3 - 3 \epsilon ^2 T(1+Tm) - \epsilon ^4 T^3\right] }{\left( (1+Tm)^3 - \epsilon ^2 T^2\right) ^3}, \end{aligned}$$which has the same sign as \( h(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0):= m(1+Tm)^3 - 3 \epsilon ^2 T(1+Tm) - \epsilon ^4 T^3. \) Observing that \(h(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \epsilon \), that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial h}{\partial m} (m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = (1+Tm)^3 + 3 Tm(1+Tm)^2 - 3 \epsilon ^2 T^2 \end{aligned}$$is increasing in m and \( \frac{\partial h}{\partial m} (\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = 4 \epsilon ^3 T^3 + 6 \epsilon ^2 T^2 + 6 \epsilon \, T + 1 >0, \) we conclude that \(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T} (m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)>0\) for \(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\). This proves Fact2. \(\square \)
Fact 3. \(\forall m,\epsilon ,m_0\), with \(m > \epsilon ,\)$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,0^+,m_0):=\lim _{T \downarrow 0} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = m_0 - m \end{aligned}$$and$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{T \uparrow +\infty } F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{1+m_0-2\epsilon }{2}, \end{aligned}$$whereas for \(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\),$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{T \uparrow +\infty } F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = 1-m. \end{aligned}$$These are simple asymptotics, and the proof is omitted.Now, we can prove Proposition 3.1. We begin with the case \(\epsilon \le m_0\). By Fact 3, \( F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,0^+,m_0) = m_0 - \epsilon \ge 0; \) thus, by Fact 2, \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)>0\), \(\forall T>0\). Since, clearly, \(F(1,\epsilon ,T,m_0)<0\) and \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly concave by Fact 1, the uniqueness of the solution to (19) follows readily and case (i) is proved.Now, consider the case \(\epsilon \ge \frac{1+m_0}{2}\) (which implies \(\epsilon >m_0\)). By Facts 2 and 3, \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)< 0\), \(\forall T>0\). Since \(F(m,\epsilon ,0^+,m_0) = m_0 - m\), by continuity (19) has no solution in \((\epsilon ,1]\) for T small enough. Now, we claim that there exists a unique \(T_c^{(1)} = T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)>0\) such that the graph of \(z= F(m,\epsilon ,T_c^{(1)},m_0)\) is tangent to the line \(z=0\). Since \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly increasing in T (Fact 2), such \(T_c^{(1)}\), if any, is unique. To prove the existence of \(T_c^{(1)}\) by continuity, it is enough to show that there are values of \(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\) and \(T>0\) such that \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)>0\): this is true \(\forall m \in (\epsilon ,1)\) as \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) \rightarrow 1-m\) for \(T \uparrow +\infty \) (Fact 3). The conclusions in case (ii) are now obvious consequences of concavity and T-monotonicity of F.Consider, finally, the case \(m_0< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\). We first investigate the following equation in the unknown \(T>0\):$$\begin{aligned} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)=0, \end{aligned}$$
                    (21)
                that can be rewritten as$$\begin{aligned} 1-\epsilon - \frac{2\epsilon ^2 T^2 + 2 \epsilon \,T + 1}{4 \epsilon ^2 T^2 + 4 \epsilon \,T + 1} (1-m_0) = 0. \end{aligned}$$This can be cast as a quadratic equation in T: it has real solutions if and only if \(\epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), and in this case the only positive solution is \(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\) given in (15). Note that \(\epsilon > m_0\) implies that \(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)>0\). Now, T-monotonicity and m-concavity of \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) and the fact that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = -1+2(1-m_0) \frac{T(\epsilon \,T+1)(4 \epsilon ^2 T^2 + 2 \epsilon \, T + 1)}{2\epsilon \, T + 1} \end{aligned}$$
                    (22)
                is strictly increasing in T, show that there are two alternatives: 

(a)
if \(\frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) \le 0\), then,

for \(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\), the map \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is less than or equal to 0 at \(m = \epsilon \) and it is decreasing: there are no solutions to (19) in \((\epsilon ,1]\);


for \(T > T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\), the map \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly positive at \(m = \epsilon \), concave and negative at \(m=1\), so (19) has a unique solution in \((\epsilon ,1]\);




(b)
if \(\frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) > 0\), then by continuity there exists \(T_c^{(1)} = T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0) \in (0,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0))\) such that the graph of \(z= F(m,\epsilon ,T_c,m_0)\) is tangent to the line \(z=0\); as above, this \(T_c^{(1)}\) is unique. Thus,

for \(T<T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\), (19) has no solutions in \((\epsilon ,1]\);


for \(T=T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\), (19) has a unique solution in \((\epsilon ,1]\);


for \(T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)<T<T^*(\epsilon , m_0)\), (19) has two solutions in \((\epsilon ,1]\);


for \(T \ge T^*(\epsilon , m_0)\), (19) has a unique solution in \((\epsilon ,1]\).



Note that \(T_c^{(1)}\) is defined as in case (ii), and by the Implicit Function Theorem it is continuous at \(\epsilon \), \(\forall \epsilon \) in its domain. To complete the proof of case (iii), we are left to show that there exists \(\epsilon _*^{(1)} \in \left( m_0,\frac{1+m_0}{2}\right) \) such that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) > 0 \ \ \hbox { if and only if } \ \ \epsilon \in \left( \epsilon _*^{(1)},\frac{1+m_0}{2}\right) , \end{aligned}$$so that \(T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\) is defined for \(\epsilon \in (\epsilon _*^{(1)},1]\). Moreover, if this is the case, continuity implies that$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{\epsilon \downarrow \epsilon _*^{(1)}} T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)= T^*(\epsilon ^*,m_0). \end{aligned}$$To complete the proof we are left to show the existence of such \(\epsilon _*^{(1)}\). This is established by proving that the map \( \epsilon \mapsto \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) \) is strictly increasing, negative at \(\epsilon = m_0\) and diverging to \(+\infty \) at \(\epsilon = \frac{1+m_0}{2}\). We use the expressions (22) and (15), and the change of variable \( y:= 1 + 2 \epsilon \, T^*(\epsilon ). \) Note that, by (15),$$\begin{aligned} \epsilon \,T^*(\epsilon ) = - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{1-m_0}{1+m_0-2\epsilon }}, \end{aligned}$$so \(y = \sqrt{\frac{1-m_0}{1+m_0-2\epsilon }}\). It is easily seen that \(\frac{dy}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } > 0\), \(y(m_0) = 1\) and \( \lim _{\epsilon \uparrow \frac{1+m_0}{2}} y(\epsilon ) = +\infty . \)We can write, using (22),$$\begin{aligned} G(\epsilon ,m_0):= \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) = -1 + (1-m_0) \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{2 \epsilon y}. \end{aligned}$$This shows that \(G(m_0,m_0) = -1\) and that \(G(\epsilon ,m_0)\) diverges to \(+\infty \) as \(\epsilon \uparrow \frac{1+m_0}{2}\). So the last step is to prove that G is strictly increasing in \(\epsilon \). We have$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial G}{\partial \epsilon }(\epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{1-m_0}{2\epsilon } \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}y} \left( \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \right) \frac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } - \frac{1-m_0}{2 \epsilon ^2} \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y}. \end{aligned}$$Using the facts that \( \frac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } = \frac{y^3}{1-m_0} \hbox { and } \epsilon = \frac{(1+m_0)y^2 - (1-m_0)}{y^2}, \) it follows that \(\frac{\partial G}{\partial \epsilon }(\epsilon ,m_0) \) has the same sign as$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} \frac{\epsilon y^3}{1-m_0} \frac{3 y^4 - 2 y^3 - y^2 + 1}{y^2} - \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \nonumber \\{} & {} \quad = \frac{\frac{1+m_0}{1-m_0} y^2 - 1}{y}(y^2(y-1)(3y+1) + 1) - \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \nonumber \\{} & {} \quad \ge \frac{ y^2 - 1}{y}(y^2(y-1)(3y+1) + 1) - \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \nonumber \\{} & {} \quad = (y^2 - 1)(y-1)(3y^2 +y - 1)> 0, \ \ \forall \, y>1, \end{aligned}$$
                    (23)
                where we have used the fact that \(m_0 \ge 0\) and the expression in the second line of (23) is increasing in \(m_0\). This completes the proof. \(\square \)1.2 Proof of Proposition 3.2: Polarized Incoherent Equilibria (\(m < -\epsilon \))The proof repeats some of the arguments seen in the proof of Proposition 3.1. It is convenient to take advantage of the symmetry relation (14). This implies that we can equivalently find the equilibria in \((\epsilon ,1]\) after replacing \(m_0\) by \(-m_0\). For the regime \(\epsilon \ge \frac{1-m_0}{2}\), the proof of part (ii) of Proposition 3.1 applies with no changes, as the assumption \(m_0 \ge 0\) was not used. For the case \(0< \epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}\), we can adapt the proof of part (iii) of Proposition 3.1, where the assumption \(m_0 \ge 0\) was only used to prove the existence of \(\epsilon _*^{(1)}\). Here, we obtain the same behavior seen for \(\epsilon _*^{(1)}< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\) in Proposition 3.1: to repeat the same argument we need to show that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0),-m_0) > 0, \ \ \ \forall \epsilon : \ \ 0< \epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}. \end{aligned}$$Indeed, as seen in the proof of Proposition 3.1,$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0),-m_0) = -1 + (1+m_0) \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{2 \epsilon y}, \end{aligned}$$with \(y = \sqrt{\frac{1+m_0}{1-m_0-2\epsilon }}\). Note that, as \(m_0 \ge 0\), \(y >1\) for all \( 0< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\). In particular, \(y^2-y+1>1\) so that, with a further simple computation we get$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0),-m_0)> -1+ (1+m_0) \frac{y^2-1}{2 \epsilon y} = -1 + \frac{\epsilon + m_0}{\epsilon }y >0. \end{aligned}$$Thus, the proof for the case \(0< \epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}\) can be carried out in the same way as the proof of part (iii) of Proposition 3.1 (case \(\epsilon _*^{(1)}< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)). \(\square \)1.3 Proof of Proposition 3.3: Unpolarized Coherent Equilibria (\(0\le m\le \epsilon \))For \(m\in [0,\epsilon ]\), equation (13) becomes:$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0):= \frac{2(\epsilon \,T+1)Tm + m_0 \left[ (\epsilon \, T+1)^2 + m^2 T^2 \right] }{\left( (\epsilon \, T+1)^2 - m^2 T^2\right) ^2} - m = 0. \end{aligned}$$
                    (24)
                We begin by observing that$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{1}{(\epsilon \, T+1)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{T}{1+\epsilon \, T} m, m_0\right) - m, \end{aligned}$$
                    (25)
                where$$\begin{aligned} \varphi (z,m_0):= \frac{2z+m_0 (1+z^2)}{(1-z^2)^2}. \end{aligned}$$
                    (26)
                In particular, this allows to prove easily that, if \(m_0 \ge 0\), then \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is convex. 

(i)
Note first that \( F(0,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{m_0}{(\epsilon \,T +1)^2} > 0 \)

(recall that here \(m_0 \ge \epsilon >0\)). Moreover,$$\begin{aligned} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{2(\epsilon \,T + 1) \epsilon \,T + m_0 \left[ (\epsilon \,T + 1) + \epsilon ^2 T^2\right] }{\left( (\epsilon \, T + 1)^2 - \epsilon ^2 T^2\right) ^2} - \epsilon = \psi (\epsilon \,T,m_0) - \epsilon , \end{aligned}$$with$$\begin{aligned} \psi (y):= \frac{2(y+1)y + m_0 \left[ (y+1)^2 + y^2 \right] }{(2y+1)^2}. \end{aligned}$$As \( \psi '(y) = \frac{2(1-m_0)}{(2y+1)^3}, \) we deduce that \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly increasing in T, except for \(m_0=1\) (in this case it is constant). In all cases, we have that, since \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,0,m_0) = m_0-\epsilon \ge 0\), \( F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) >0, \) \(\forall T>0\). Thus, the map \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly positive at the endpoints of the interval \((0,\epsilon )\). We also have \( F(m,\epsilon ,0,m_0) = m_0 - m >0 \) \(\forall m \in (0,\epsilon )\). Moreover, it is easily seen that for each \(m \in (0,\epsilon )\),$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = -m <0. \end{aligned}$$
                    (27)
                Then, there must be a time \(T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\) such that for \(T < T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\), \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) > 0\), \(\forall m \in (0,\epsilon )\), and the graph of the convex function \(y = F(m,\epsilon ,T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0)\) is tangent to the line \(y=0\). Set$$\begin{aligned} {\mathcal {T}}:= \left\{ T > 0: \min _{m \in (0,\epsilon )}F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) \le 0 \right\} . \end{aligned}$$The proof of this point (i) is completed as we show that \({\mathcal {T}} = [T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0), +\infty )\). If this is not the case, by continuity, there must be a time \({\hat{T}} \ge T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\), \({\hat{m}} \in (0,\epsilon )\) and \(\delta >0\) such that$$\begin{aligned} F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,{\hat{T}},m_0) = 0, \ \ \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}m}F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,{\hat{T}},m_0) = 0, \end{aligned}$$
                    (28)
                but \( F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,t,m_0)>0, \) \(\forall t \in ({\hat{T}}, {\hat{T}}+\delta )\). To show that this is impossible, it is enough to prove that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}T}F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,{\hat{T}},m_0) <0. \end{aligned}$$
                    (29)
                To see this, it is convenient to perform the following change of variables: \(u:= \frac{m}{\epsilon } \in (0,1)\) and \(r:= \epsilon \, T\), so that$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) - \epsilon u, \end{aligned}$$
                    (30)
                where \(\varphi \) is given in (26). Note that (29) is equivalent to$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}r} G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) <0, \end{aligned}$$
                    (31)
                where \({\hat{u}}:= \frac{{\hat{m}}}{\epsilon }\) and \({\hat{r}}:= \epsilon {\hat{T}}\).
Claim
\({\hat{r}} > \frac{1}{2}\).
Proof
To see this, note that, being \(\epsilon \le m_0\),$$\begin{aligned} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) \ge H(u,r,m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) - m_0 u. \end{aligned}$$The claim follows if we show that, \(\forall r \le \frac{1}{2}\),$$\begin{aligned} H(u,r,m_0) > 0, \ \ \forall u \in (0,1). \end{aligned}$$
                    (32)
                Since \(H(u,r,m_0)\) is linear in \(m_0\), it is enough to prove (32) for \(m_0\in \{0,1\}\). For \(m_0 = 0\) this is obvious, so we show it for \(m_0 = 1\):$$\begin{aligned} H(u,r,1) = \frac{\left( 1+ \frac{r}{1+r} u \right) ^2}{(1+r)^2 \left( 1- \frac{r^2 u^2}{(1+r)^2} \right) ^2} - u = \frac{(1-u)(r^2 u^2 - (r^2 +2r)u+1)}{(1+r(1-u))^2}, \end{aligned}$$which has the same sign as \( p(u):= r^2 u^2 - (r^2 +2r)u+1). \) If \(r \le \frac{1}{2}\) (indeed here \(r<2\) would suffice), \(p'(u) = 2 r^2 u - r^2 - 2r < 0\), \(\forall u \in (0,1)\), so, \( p(u) \ge p(1) = 1-2r \ge 0, \) \(\forall u \in (0,1)\) and for \(r \le \frac{1}{2}\). This completes the proof of the Claim. \(\square \)
We are now left with the proof of (31). Note that$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}r}G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = - \frac{2}{(1+r)^3} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) + \frac{u}{(1+r)^4} \varphi '\left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) , \end{aligned}$$
                    (33)
                where \( \varphi '(z,m_0) = \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}z} \varphi (z,m_0). \) By (28),$$\begin{aligned} G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) = 0 \ \Rightarrow \ \varphi \left( \frac{{\hat{r}}}{1+{\hat{r}}} {\hat{u}}, m_0\right) = \epsilon {\hat{u}}, \end{aligned}$$and$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u}G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) = 0 \ \Rightarrow \ \frac{r}{(1+r)^3} \varphi '\left( \frac{{\hat{r}}}{1+{\hat{r}}} {\hat{u}}, m_0\right) = \epsilon . \end{aligned}$$Inserting these identities in (33), we obtain$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}r}G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) = \frac{\epsilon {\hat{u}}}{1+{\hat{r}}}\left( -2+\frac{1}{{\hat{r}}} \right) <0, \quad \text { as } {\hat{r}} > \frac{1}{2}. \end{aligned}$$

(ii)
Note that \(F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{m_0}{(\epsilon \, T+1)^2} >0\). Moreover, \( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{1+m_0}{2} - \epsilon \le 0. \) Since, as shown in point (i), the map \(T \mapsto F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) \) is strictly increasing, then \( F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) <0, \) \(\forall T >0\). Thus, \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\) has opposite sign at the endpoints of \((0,\epsilon )\): by convexity there is a unique solution \(m = M(T, \epsilon ,m_0)\) to \(F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0\). The fact that \( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } M(T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \) follows from the fact that \( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \) and that \( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}m} F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = -1. \)


(iii)
In this case, \(F(0,T, \epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{m_0}{(\epsilon \, T +1)^2} \ge 0\). Moreover, \(F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0)< 0 \ \iff \ T < T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),\) as already seen in the proof of Proposition 3.1, point (iii). By convexity of \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\), for \(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\) Eq. (24) has a unique solution in \([0,\epsilon )\). The fact that \(m=0\) is a solution if and only if \(m_0=0\) is easily verified. Moreover, by (27), for T sufficiently large \(F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\) attains negative values, it is positive at \(m=0\) and \(m=\epsilon \), so (24) has two solutions in \([0,\epsilon )\). We need, however, a sharper analysis for \(T> T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\). Note that, in this case \(F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) > 0\) and \(F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) >0\). Thus, by convexity of \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\), (24) has zero or two solutions, except for the “special times” T for which the graph of the map \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\) is tangent to the horizontal axis. Note that these special times are identified as (T-component of the) solutions in \((0,\epsilon ) \times (T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),+\infty )\) of the system $$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \\ \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}m}F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$
                    (34)
                 Note that (34) has no solutions with \(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\), so we may look for solutions of (34) in \((0,\epsilon ) \times (0,+\infty )\). The remaining part of the proof is based on the following Lemma.


Lemma A.1
Let \(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\) and \(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\) be as in the statement of Proposition 3.3. Then,$$\begin{aligned} m_0< \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)< \epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0) < \frac{1+m_0}{2}, \end{aligned}$$(unless for \(m_0=0\), where \(0 = \epsilon _*^{(2)}(0)< \epsilon _*^{(3)}(0) < \frac{1}{2}\)) such that 

(a)
for \(m_0 < \epsilon \le \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\), (34) has a unique solution \(({\tilde{m}}(\epsilon ,m _0), {\tilde{T}}(\epsilon ,m_0))\);


(b)
for \(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)< \epsilon <\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\), (34) has two solutions \(({\hat{m}}(\epsilon ,m _0), T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0))\) and \(({\tilde{m}}(\epsilon ,m _0), T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0))\) with \( T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)< T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\);


(c)
for \( \epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0) \le \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), (34) has no solutions.


The proof of this lemma is postponed after the end of this section. The desired result of the solutions of (24) readily follows from this Lemma. Indeed, in case (a), there is a unique special time \(T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\). Since, for large T, (24) has two solutions, necessarily for \(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0) < T \le T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\) we must have \(F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)>0\), \(\forall m \in (0,\epsilon )\), so (24) has no solution.In case (b), there are two special times \( T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)< T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\): the only possibility is that, for \(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)< T < T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\), the graph of \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\) crosses twice the horizontal axis (two solutions for (24)), for \(T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)< T<T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\) it stays above the horizontal axis (no solutions for (24)) and it crosses again twice the horizontal axis for \(T > T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\). The proof is therefore completed. \(\square \)1.4 Proof of Lemma A.1
By the change of variables \(u:= \frac{m}{\epsilon } \in (0,1)\) and \(r:= \epsilon \, T\) as in (30), we may replace \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) by$$\begin{aligned} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r+ru)^2} \left[ m_0 + \frac{2(1+m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \right] - \epsilon u =0, \end{aligned}$$\(u \in (0,1)\), \(r >0\), and (34) is equivalent to$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0 \\ \frac{\text {d}}{\text {d}u} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$
                    (35)
                Letting, as above, \( U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= m_0(1+r-ru)^2 + 2(1+m_0)r(1+r)u - \epsilon u\left[ (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right] ^2 = 0, \) we have that \( G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = \frac{1}{\left[ (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right] ^2} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0). \) It is immediately seen that (35) is equivalent to$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0 \\ \frac{\text {d}}{\text {d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0, \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$
                    (36)
                We use again the identity \( U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) +\left( m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) \left( (1+r)^2 - u^2 r^2 \right) , \) which implies that (36) is equivalent to$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \left( m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) = 0 \\ \frac{\text {d}}{\text {d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$
                    (37)
                Summing up, the number of pairs \((m,T) \in (0,\epsilon ) \times (0,+\infty )\) solving (34) equals the number of solutions r to the equation$$\begin{aligned} W(r,\epsilon ,m_0):= \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U\left( \left( \frac{m_0}{4 \epsilon r^2} \right) ^{1/3},\epsilon ,r,m_0\right) = 0 \end{aligned}$$
                    (38)
                such that \(\frac{m_0}{4 \epsilon r^2} <1\), i.e., \(r> \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }}\). With the help of a symbolic calculator, we obtain$$\begin{aligned} 8W(r,\epsilon ,m_0)= & {} r \left\{ 16 + \left[ 16 + 3m_0 \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon r^2} \right) ^{1/3} \right] r \right\} \\{} & {} \quad + 4 \epsilon (1+r)^2 \left\{ -2-4r+ \left[ -2 + 3 \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon r^2} \right) ^{1/3}\right] r^2 \right\} . \end{aligned}$$Now, set \( V(s,\epsilon ,m_0):= W(\sqrt{s},\epsilon ,m_0). \) Thus, we are left with the problem of finding the solutions of$$\begin{aligned} V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \end{aligned}$$
                    (39)
                with \(s > \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }\). This last change of variable is a trick to get the following claim.
Claim
The map \(s \mapsto V(s,\epsilon ,m_0)\) is strictly concave in \(\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon },+\infty \right) \).
Proof
Using again a symbolic calculator we get, letting \(k:= \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{1/3}\),$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} -12 s^{3/2} \frac{\textrm{d}^2}{\textrm{d}s^2}V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) \\{} & {} \qquad = 6+m_0 k s^{1/6} + \epsilon \left[ - 12 + 4 k^2 s^{-1/6} + 36 s + 5 k^2 s^{1/3} + 24 s^{3/2} - 8 k^2 s^{5/6} \right] . \end{aligned}$$To prove the claim, we need to show that this last expression is positive. Note that \(s > \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon } = \left( \frac{k}{2}\right) ^3\). Moreover, as \(m_0< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), then \( \frac{4m_0}{1+m_0}< k^3 < 2. \) Again from \(\epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), the inequality \( \frac{\textrm{d}^2}{\textrm{d}s^2}V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) <0\) follows if we show that$$\begin{aligned} 6+m_0 k s^{1/6} + \frac{1+m_0}{2} \left[ - 12 + 4 k^2 s^{-1/6} + 36 s + 5 k^2 s^{1/3} + 24 s^{3/2} - 8 k^2 s^{5/6} \right] >0. \end{aligned}$$
                    (40)
                Using \(s > \left( \frac{k}{2}\right) ^3\) and \(k^3 > \frac{4m_0}{1+m_0}\), we have that \( \frac{1+m_0}{2}5 k^2\,s^{1/3}>\frac{1+m_0}{4}5k^3> 5m_0, \) so the l.h.s. of (40) is bounded from below by$$\begin{aligned} m_0 k s^{1/6} - m_0 + (1+m_0) \left[ 2k^2 s^{-1/6} + 18 s + 12 s^{3/2} - 4 k^2 s^{5/6} \right] . \end{aligned}$$
                    (41)
                Now, for \(s \le \frac{1}{4}\) the expression in (41) is bounded from below by$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} - m_0 + (1+m_0) \left[ 2k^2 s^{-1/6} - 4 k^2 s^{5/6} \right]&= -m_0 +\frac{1+m_0}{s^{1/6}} \left( 2k^2 - 4 k^2 s \right) \\ {}&\ge - m_0 +(1+m_0) k^2 s^{-1/6} \\ {}&\ge - m_0 +(1+m_0) \left( \frac{4 m_0}{1+m_0} \right) ^{2/3} \\ {}&\ge - m_0 + (1+m_0) 4^{2/3} \frac{m_0}{1+m_0} >0. \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$For \(s > \frac{1}{4}\), the expression in (41) is bounded from below by$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} - m_0 + 18 s + 12 s^{3/2} - 4 k^2 s^{5/6}&\ge -m_0 + s^{5/6} \left[ 18 \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{1/6} + 12 \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{2/3} - 4 k^2 \right] \\ {}&\ge -m_0 + s^{5/6} \left[ \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{1/6} + 12 \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{2/3} - 4 \, 2^{2/3} \right] \\ {}&\ge -m_0 + 8s^{5/6} \ge -m_0 + 2 > 0. \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$This proves (40) and therefore the claim. \(\square \)
Now, using concavity of \(V(s,\epsilon ,m_0)\) and the fact that \( \lim _{s \rightarrow +\infty } V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) = \lim _{r \rightarrow +\infty } W(r,\epsilon ,m_0) = -\infty , \) we have that (39) has a unique solution whenever \( V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) >0. \) We get$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) = \frac{m_0(1+m_0)}{2\epsilon } + (1+m_0) \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }} - \epsilon \left( 1+e \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }} \right) , \end{aligned}$$so$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) \Big |_{\epsilon = m_0} = \frac{3}{2}(1-m_0) >0, \end{aligned}$$$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) \Big |_{\epsilon = \frac{(1+m_0)}{2}} = -\frac{1}{2}(1-m_0) < 0 \end{aligned}$$
                    (42)
                and$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) = -1- \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }}-\frac{m_0(1+m_0)}{2\epsilon ^2} - \frac{ \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }}(1+m_0)}{2\epsilon } < 0. \end{aligned}$$Therefore, there is a unique \(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\), with \(m_0< \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0) < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), such that \(V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon _*^{(2)}}, \epsilon _*^{(2)}, m_0\right) = 0\). Moreover, for \(m_0< \epsilon < \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\), (39) has a unique solution and, since$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}s} V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) \Big |_{s = \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }} = \frac{2(1+m_0-2\epsilon ) \left( 1+\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\alpha }}\right) }{\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\alpha }}} >0, \end{aligned}$$it follows that (39) has two solutions as \(\epsilon \) crosses \(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\). This actually occurs until \(\epsilon \) reaches \(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\), where \(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\) is characterized by the fact that the graph of \(s \mapsto V(s,\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0),m_0)\) is tangent to the horizontal axis. This is a consequence of the following monotonicity property:$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) <0,\quad \forall s \ge \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }. \end{aligned}$$
                    (43)
                This suffices to characterize \(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\); to complete the proof, we need to show that \(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0) < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\), which is equivalent to$$\begin{aligned} V\left( s,\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) <0,\quad \forall s \ge \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)}. \end{aligned}$$
                    (44)
                We are therefore left to prove (43) and (44). We begin with (43).$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) \\{} & {} \quad =-\frac{1}{8\epsilon } \left[ m_0 \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{1/3} s^{2/3} + 4 \epsilon (1+\sqrt{s})^2 \left( 2 + 4 \sqrt{s} + 4 s -\left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{2/3} s^{1/3}\right) \right] . \end{aligned}$$To show that this expression is negative, we just observe that, being \(\frac{2m_0}{\epsilon } \le 2\),$$\begin{aligned} 2 + 4 \sqrt{s} + 4 s -\left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{2/3} s^{1/3}> 2 + 4 \sqrt{s} + 4 s -2 s^{1/3}> \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} 2-2s^{1/3} \ge 0 &{} \hbox {for } s \le 1 \\ 4s - 2 s^{1/3}> 0 &{} \hbox {for } s>1. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$This establishes (43). Now, we show (44). We recall that \(s \mapsto V\left( s,\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) \) is strictly concave for \(s \ge \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)}\). Moreover, again with the help of a symbolic calculator$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}s} V\left( s,\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) \Big |_{s = \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)}} = 0. \end{aligned}$$So it is enough to show that$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)},\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) <0, \end{aligned}$$that has been seen already in (42). The proof is now complete. \(\square \)1.5 Proof of Proposition 3.4: Unpolarized Incoherent Equilibria (\(-\epsilon \le m < 0\))As done in Proposition 3.2, we use the symmetry (14). So we look for solutions \(m \in (0,\epsilon ]\) of the equation \( F(m,\epsilon ,T,-m_0) = 0; \) this allows to reuse some of the ideas in Proposition 3.3. 

(i)
We employ here the change of variables seen in (30), so $$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,-m_0)&= G(u,\epsilon ,r,-m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, -m_0\right) - \epsilon u \\ {}&= \frac{2(1+r)ru-m_0\left[ (1+r)^2+r^2 u^2\right] }{\left( (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right) ^2} - \epsilon u \\&= \frac{1}{(1+r+ru)^2} \left[ -m_0 + \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \right] - \epsilon u. \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$ We need to show that this last expression is strictly negative \(\forall u\in (0,1)\); it is enough to show this for \(\epsilon = \frac{1-m_0}{2}\). This amounts to prove that $$\begin{aligned} -m_0 + \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} < \frac{1-m_0}{2} u (1+r+ru)^2, \end{aligned}$$ which follows from $$\begin{aligned}{} & {} \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \\{} & {} \quad< \frac{1-m_0}{2} u (1+r+ru)^2 \\{} & {} \qquad \iff \ 4r(1+r) < \left[ (1+r)^2 - r^2 u^2 \right] ^2, \end{aligned}$$\(\forall u \in (0,1)\). This last inequality follows if we show it holds for \(u=1\), i.e., \( 4r(1+r) < (1+2r)^2, \) that is clearly true \(\forall r>0\).


(ii)
As seen in the proof of point (i) of Proposition 3.3, \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,-m_0)\) is strictly increasing in T. Moreover, the same computation done after (21) shows that \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,-m_0)>0\) if and only if \(T>T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\). By the same change of variables used in point (i), (24) is equivalent to the equation $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{(1+r+ru)^2} \left[ -m_0 + \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \right] - \epsilon u =0, \end{aligned}$$ with \(u \in (0,1)\), which is also equivalent to $$\begin{aligned}{} & {} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= -m_0(1+r-ru)^2 \\{} & {} \qquad + 2(1-m_0)r(1+r)u - \epsilon u\left[ (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right] ^2 = 0. \end{aligned}$$ Our proof is based on the following claim.


Claim
Let \(u^* \in (0,1)\) be such that \(U(u^*,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0\). Then, \( \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u^*,\epsilon ,r,m_0)>0. \)
Proof
We use the identity$$\begin{aligned} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0)= & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) +\left( -m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) \left( (1+r)^2 - u^2 r^2 \right) \\< & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) \end{aligned}$$which proves the claim. \(\square \)
This clearly implies that such \(u^*\) exists if and only if \(U(1,\epsilon ,r,m_0) >0\) (as \(U(0,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = -m_0(1+r)^2\le 0\)), i.e., if and only if \(r > \epsilon \, T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\) and in this case it is unique. This completes the proof. \(\square \)

text_blocks.text: 1.1 Proof of Proposition 3.1: Polarized Coherent Equilibria (\(m > \epsilon \))
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: Before describing the solutions of the equation \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) \) in \((\epsilon ,1]\), we give some simple facts that will be useful in the proof.

text_blocks.text: Fact 1. The map \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly concave for \(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\), for each \(\epsilon ,T,m_0\).

text_blocks.text: Fact 2. The map \(T \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly increasing for \(T \in [0,+\infty )\), for each \(m,\epsilon ,m_0\), with \(m > \epsilon \).

This proves Fact2. \(\square \)

text_blocks.text: Now, we can prove Proposition 3.1. We begin with the case \(\epsilon \le m_0\). By Fact 3, \( F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,0^+,m_0) = m_0 - \epsilon \ge 0; \) thus, by Fact 2, \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)>0\), \(\forall T>0\). Since, clearly, \(F(1,\epsilon ,T,m_0)<0\) and \(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly concave by Fact 1, the uniqueness of the solution to (19) follows readily and case (i) is proved.
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: Now, consider the case \(\epsilon \ge \frac{1+m_0}{2}\) (which implies \(\epsilon >m_0\)). By Facts 2 and 3, \(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)< 0\), \(\forall T>0\). Since \(F(m,\epsilon ,0^+,m_0) = m_0 - m\), by continuity (19) has no solution in \((\epsilon ,1]\) for T small enough. Now, we claim that there exists a unique \(T_c^{(1)} = T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)>0\) such that the graph of \(z= F(m,\epsilon ,T_c^{(1)},m_0)\) is tangent to the line \(z=0\). Since \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\) is strictly increasing in T (Fact 2), such \(T_c^{(1)}\), if any, is unique. To prove the existence of \(T_c^{(1)}\) by continuity, it is enough to show that there are values of \(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\) and \(T>0\) such that \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)>0\): this is true \(\forall m \in (\epsilon ,1)\) as \(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) \rightarrow 1-m\) for \(T \uparrow +\infty \) (Fact 3). The conclusions in case (ii) are now obvious consequences of concavity and T-monotonicity of F.

Moreover, \( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{1+m_0}{2} - \epsilon \le 0. \) Since, as shown in point (i), the map \(T \mapsto F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) \) is strictly increasing, then \( F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) <0, \) \(\forall T >0\). Thus, \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\) has opposite sign at the endpoints of \((0,\epsilon )\): by convexity there is a unique solution \(m = M(T, \epsilon ,m_0)\) to \(F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0\). In this case, \(F(0,T, \epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{m_0}{(\epsilon \, T +1)^2} \ge 0\). Moreover, \(F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0)< 0 \ \iff \ T < T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),\) as already seen in the proof of Proposition 3.1, point (iii). By convexity of \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\), for \(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\) Eq. (24) has a unique solution in \([0,\epsilon )\). The fact that \(m=0\) is a solution if and only if \(m_0=0\) is easily verified. Moreover, by (27), for T sufficiently large \(F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\) attains negative values, it is positive at \(m=0\) and \(m=\epsilon \), so (24) has two solutions in \([0,\epsilon )\). We need, however, a sharper analysis for \(T> T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\). Note that, in this case \(F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) > 0\) and \(F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) >0\). Thus, by convexity of \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\), (24) has zero or two solutions, except for the “special times” T for which the graph of the map \(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\) is tangent to the horizontal axis. Note that these special times are identified as (T-component of the) solutions in \((0,\epsilon ) \times (T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),+\infty )\) of the system $$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \\ \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}m}F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$

text_blocks.text: with \(u \in (0,1)\), which is also equivalent to
text_blocks.label: other

text_blocks.text: Our proof is based on the following claim.
text_blocks.label: other

text_blocks.text: Let \(u^* \in (0,1)\) be such that \(U(u^*,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0\). Then, \( \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u^*,\epsilon ,r,m_0)>0. \)
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: $$\begin{aligned} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0)= & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) +\left( -m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) \left( (1+r)^2 - u^2 r^2 \right) \\< & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) \end{aligned}$$
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: $$\begin{aligned} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0)= & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) +\left( -m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) \left( (1+r)^2 - u^2 r^2 \right) \\< & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) \end{aligned}$$
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: $$\begin{aligned} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0)= & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) +\left( -m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) \left( (1+r)^2 - u^2 r^2 \right) \\< & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) \end{aligned}$$
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: This clearly implies that such \(u^*\) exists if and only if \(U(1,\epsilon ,r,m_0) >0\) (as \(U(0,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = -m_0(1+r)^2\le 0\)), i.e., if and only if \(r > \epsilon \, T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\) and in this case it is unique. This completes the proof. \(\square \)
